title: Perthes Disease
oneLiner: Idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head in children (usually 4–8 years, ♂>♀); causes limp and hip pain – early diagnosis improves long-term outcomes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic arthritis of the hip** – a surgical emergency that destroys the femoral head in hours.  
  *Single finding:* Fever + inability to bear weight + hip held in flexion/abduction/external rotation (irritable hip but with high CRP/PCT).
- **Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE)** – requires urgent pinning to prevent avascular necrosis.  
  *Single finding:* Acute onset limp in an older child (10–15 years) with external rotation on hip flexion (obligatory external rotation).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painless limp (early) → becomes painful over weeks–months
  - Hip, groin, or referred knee pain (classic: “knee pain” in a child with hip disease)
  - Reduced activity, avoiding weight‑bearing on affected side
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Antalgic gait
  - Restricted active and **passive** range of motion: **loss of abduction and internal rotation** most specific
  - Thigh/calf muscle atrophy (chronic)
  - Trendelenburg gait (gluteal weakness) in advanced cases
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Transient synovitis (“irritable hip”)** – self‑limiting, normal X‑ray/ultrasound, low CRP; resolves in days with rest.
  - **Septic arthritis** – fever >38°C, severe pain, unable to bear weight, CRP often >20 mg/L, hip ultrasound shows effusion + positive culture/blood markers.
  - **SCFE** – older child (10–15), positive Klein’s line on X‑ray, external rotation on flexion.
  - **Juvenile idiopathic arthritis** – morning stiffness, multiple joints, uveitis, systemic features (rash, fever).
- **Splitter:** “Hip held in flexion/abduction/external rotation with fever → septic arthritis; internally rotated at rest → Perthes or SCFE.”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - None specific; ensure child is not septic (NEWS2/PEWS)
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, CRP, ESR** – to rule out infection (septic arthritis if CRP >20–30 mg/L, but no validated cutoff)
  - Blood cultures if fever or high CRP
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** A‑P pelvis + frog‑leg lateral hip X‑ray – look for **crescent sign** (subchondral fracture), **widened medial joint space**, **fragmentation**, **loss of femoral head height** (early: X‑ray may be normal)
  - **Second‑line:** **MRI hip** (gold standard) – shows marrow oedema and necrosis before X‑ray changes; consider if X‑ray normal but high clinical suspicion
  - Ultrasound: for joint effusion (to differentiate synovitis/septic arthritis), but not diagnostic for Perthes
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone scan (rarely needed) – increased uptake in early stage, decreased in late stage
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Catterall classification** (I–IV based on extent of femoral head involvement): guides prognosis but less used now
  - **Herring lateral pillar classification** (A, B, C based on height of lateral pillar): predicts outcome – better with ≥50% pillar height
- **Never‑miss test:** **Hip X‑ray (two views) + CRP/ESR** – failure to image a limping child delays diagnosis of SCFE or septic arthritis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Analgesia (paracetamol ± ibuprofen) – see Drugs & Doses.
  - **Non‑weight‑bearing** (crutches or wheelchair) to reduce pain and protect femoral head.
  - **Urgent orthopaedic referral** if suspicion of septic arthritis, SCFE, or definitive diagnosis of Perthes.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with MRI if X‑ray equivocal.
  - Start physiotherapy: passive range‑of‑motion exercises to maintain abduction/internal rotation.
  - Activity modification: avoid running, high‑impact sports.
  - Refer to paediatric orthopaedic specialist for stage‑specific management:
    - Early disease (Catterall I–II): observation, NSAIDs, physiotherapy.
    - Later disease (Catterall III–IV or Herring B/C): consider **containment surgery** (e.g., femoral osteotomy, pelvic osteotomy) – urgent if femoral head subluxation.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Regular clinical and radiographic follow‑up (every 6–12 months) until skeletal maturity.
  - Physiotherapy for gait re‑education, strengthening.
  - Avoidance of obesity (excess weight worsens hip mechanics).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** nil‑by‑mouth if sedation/surgery planned; otherwise oral hydration encouraged.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not indicated in children unless co‑morbidities (adult dose [check local policy]).
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet unless surgery.
  - **Observation:** PEWS (Paediatric Early Warning Score) q4h; if febrile or worsening pain → q1h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** (oral/IV): 15 mg/kg/dose q4–6h (max 60 mg/kg/24h) – weight‑based.
  - **Ibuprofen** (oral): 5–10 mg/kg/dose q6–8h (max 30 mg/kg/24h) – only if no contraindications (renal, asthma, or G6PD deficiency). *[Check local policy for age cut‑offs]*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Naproxen** (oral) – 5–7.5 mg/kg/dose q12h (max 500 mg/dose) – for sustained pain; gastritis risk.
  - **Diazepam** (oral/IV) – for muscle spasm (if severe, e.g., before physiotherapy): 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/dose (max 5 mg/dose).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid NSAIDs in dehydration, renal impairment, or varicella (risk of necrotising fasciitis).
  - Paracetamol safe in most, but avoid >60 mg/kg/24h (hepatotoxicity).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m²; reduce dose in mild–moderate impairment.
  - Paracetamol: no dose adjustment unless severe hepatic impairment (avoid in active liver disease).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Ibuprofen – using adult dose (200–400 mg) in a child** → risk of acute kidney injury, gastrointestinal bleeding; always calculate per kg.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Femoral head fracture** (subchondral) – may present as sudden worsening pain.
  - **Hip subluxation/dislocation** – rare, requires urgent ortho.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Coxa magna** (enlarged femoral head) + **coxa plana** (flattening) → **secondary osteoarthritis** by adulthood (50–60% risk).
  - **Leg length discrepancy** (due to growth arrest) → require shoe lift.
  - **Abductor lurch** (Trendelenburg gait) – permanent gluteal weakness.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** nil directly from Perthes.
- **Recovery:** ~50% have good outcome (pain‑free, full range) with early containment; worse if onset >8 years, extensive necrosis (Catterall IV, Herring C), or loss of containment.
- **Chronicity:** most require decades of follow‑up; osteoarthritis develops in ~50% by age 50.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document range of motion (abduction, internal rotation) on both sides** – loss of abduction is the earliest sign of containment failure.
- **If X‑ray is normal but limp persists >2 weeks, request an MRI** – early Perthes is invisible on plain film.
- **Teach parents to supervise non‑weight‑bearing** – “your child can walk, but try to keep them off the sore leg; crutches help.”
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume “growing pains” or “synovitis” without imaging** – missing SCFE or septic arthritis is medicolegal.
- **Do not give NSAIDs for >1 week before ruling out septic arthritis** – masking fever and pain can delay diagnosis of infection.
- **Do not let a child resume sports until orthopaedic clearance** – premature weight‑bearing can deform the femoral head.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever >38°C or systemic symptoms.
  - Inability to bear weight (even partial) after 2 days of analgesics.
  - Worsening pain (especially night pain) or new “click”/“pop” sensation.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Orthopaedic clinic within 2 weeks.
  - If no appointment arranged, GP should refer urgently to paediatric orthopaedics.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ortho reg / consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspicion of septic arthritis (fever, high CRP, toxic child) – needs urgent aspiration.
  - Suspicion of SCFE (acute limp + external rotation on flexion) – needs urgent X‑ray and pinning.
  - NEWS2/PEWS ≥4 or clinical deterioration (e.g., septic shock).
  - New neurological deficit or cauda equina symptoms (rare, but consider spinal pathology).
- **ICU referral:** if septic shock or requiring HDU monitoring post‑surgery (e.g., pelvic osteotomy).

rawMarkdown:
## Perthes Disease
**One-liner:** Idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head in children (usually 4–8 years, ♂>♀); causes limp and hip pain – early diagnosis improves long-term outcomes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic arthritis of the hip** – a surgical emergency that destroys the femoral head in hours.  
  *Single finding:* Fever + inability to bear weight + hip held in flexion/abduction/external rotation (irritable hip but with high CRP/PCT).
- **Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE)** – requires urgent pinning to prevent avascular necrosis.  
  *Single finding:* Acute onset limp in an older child (10–15 years) with external rotation on hip flexion (obligatory external rotation).

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painless limp (early) → becomes painful over weeks–months
  - Hip, groin, or referred knee pain (classic: “knee pain” in a child with hip disease)
  - Reduced activity, avoiding weight‑bearing on affected side
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Antalgic gait
  - Restricted active and **passive** range of motion: **loss of abduction and internal rotation** most specific
  - Thigh/calf muscle atrophy (chronic)
  - Trendelenburg gait (gluteal weakness) in advanced cases

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Transient synovitis (“irritable hip”)** – self‑limiting, normal X‑ray/ultrasound, low CRP; resolves in days with rest.
  - **Septic arthritis** – fever >38°C, severe pain, unable to bear weight, CRP often >20 mg/L, hip ultrasound shows effusion + positive culture/blood markers.
  - **SCFE** – older child (10–15), positive Klein’s line on X‑ray, external rotation on flexion.
  - **Juvenile idiopathic arthritis** – morning stiffness, multiple joints, uveitis, systemic features (rash, fever).
- **Splitter:** “Hip held in flexion/abduction/external rotation with fever → septic arthritis; internally rotated at rest → Perthes or SCFE.”

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - None specific; ensure child is not septic (NEWS2/PEWS)
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, CRP, ESR** – to rule out infection (septic arthritis if CRP >20–30 mg/L, but no validated cutoff)
  - Blood cultures if fever or high CRP
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** A‑P pelvis + frog‑leg lateral hip X‑ray – look for **crescent sign** (subchondral fracture), **widened medial joint space**, **fragmentation**, **loss of femoral head height** (early: X‑ray may be normal)
  - **Second‑line:** **MRI hip** (gold standard) – shows marrow oedema and necrosis before X‑ray changes; consider if X‑ray normal but high clinical suspicion
  - Ultrasound: for joint effusion (to differentiate synovitis/septic arthritis), but not diagnostic for Perthes
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone scan (rarely needed) – increased uptake in early stage, decreased in late stage
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Catterall classification** (I–IV based on extent of femoral head involvement): guides prognosis but less used now
  - **Herring lateral pillar classification** (A, B, C based on height of lateral pillar): predicts outcome – better with ≥50% pillar height
- **Never‑miss test:** **Hip X‑ray (two views) + CRP/ESR** – failure to image a limping child delays diagnosis of SCFE or septic arthritis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Analgesia (paracetamol ± ibuprofen) – see Drugs & Doses.
  - **Non‑weight‑bearing** (crutches or wheelchair) to reduce pain and protect femoral head.
  - **Urgent orthopaedic referral** if suspicion of septic arthritis, SCFE, or definitive diagnosis of Perthes.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with MRI if X‑ray equivocal.
  - Start physiotherapy: passive range‑of‑motion exercises to maintain abduction/internal rotation.
  - Activity modification: avoid running, high‑impact sports.
  - Refer to paediatric orthopaedic specialist for stage‑specific management:
    - Early disease (Catterall I–II): observation, NSAIDs, physiotherapy.
    - Later disease (Catterall III–IV or Herring B/C): consider **containment surgery** (e.g., femoral osteotomy, pelvic osteotomy) – urgent if femoral head subluxation.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Regular clinical and radiographic follow‑up (every 6–12 months) until skeletal maturity.
  - Physiotherapy for gait re‑education, strengthening.
  - Avoidance of obesity (excess weight worsens hip mechanics).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** nil‑by‑mouth if sedation/surgery planned; otherwise oral hydration encouraged.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not indicated in children unless co‑morbidities (adult dose [check local policy]).
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet unless surgery.
  - **Observation:** PEWS (Paediatric Early Warning Score) q4h; if febrile or worsening pain → q1h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** (oral/IV): 15 mg/kg/dose q4–6h (max 60 mg/kg/24h) – weight‑based.
  - **Ibuprofen** (oral): 5–10 mg/kg/dose q6–8h (max 30 mg/kg/24h) – only if no contraindications (renal, asthma, or G6PD deficiency). *[Check local policy for age cut‑offs]*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Naproxen** (oral) – 5–7.5 mg/kg/dose q12h (max 500 mg/dose) – for sustained pain; gastritis risk.
  - **Diazepam** (oral/IV) – for muscle spasm (if severe, e.g., before physiotherapy): 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/dose (max 5 mg/dose).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid NSAIDs in dehydration, renal impairment, or varicella (risk of necrotising fasciitis).
  - Paracetamol safe in most, but avoid >60 mg/kg/24h (hepatotoxicity).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m²; reduce dose in mild–moderate impairment.
  - Paracetamol: no dose adjustment unless severe hepatic impairment (avoid in active liver disease).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Ibuprofen – using adult dose (200–400 mg) in a child** → risk of acute kidney injury, gastrointestinal bleeding; always calculate per kg.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Femoral head fracture** (subchondral) – may present as sudden worsening pain.
  - **Hip subluxation/dislocation** – rare, requires urgent ortho.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Coxa magna** (enlarged femoral head) + **coxa plana** (flattening) → **secondary osteoarthritis** by adulthood (50–60% risk).
  - **Leg length discrepancy** (due to growth arrest) → require shoe lift.
  - **Abductor lurch** (Trendelenburg gait) – permanent gluteal weakness.

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** nil directly from Perthes.
- **Recovery:** ~50% have good outcome (pain‑free, full range) with early containment; worse if onset >8 years, extensive necrosis (Catterall IV, Herring C), or loss of containment.
- **Chronicity:** most require decades of follow‑up; osteoarthritis develops in ~50% by age 50.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document range of motion (abduction, internal rotation) on both sides** – loss of abduction is the earliest sign of containment failure.
- **If X‑ray is normal but limp persists >2 weeks, request an MRI** – early Perthes is invisible on plain film.
- **Teach parents to supervise non‑weight‑bearing** – “your child can walk, but try to keep them off the sore leg; crutches help.”

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume “growing pains” or “synovitis” without imaging** – missing SCFE or septic arthritis is medicolegal.
- **Do not give NSAIDs for >1 week before ruling out septic arthritis** – masking fever and pain can delay diagnosis of infection.
- **Do not let a child resume sports until orthopaedic clearance** – premature weight‑bearing can deform the femoral head.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever >38°C or systemic symptoms.
  - Inability to bear weight (even partial) after 2 days of analgesics.
  - Worsening pain (especially night pain) or new “click”/“pop” sensation.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Orthopaedic clinic within 2 weeks.
  - If no appointment arranged, GP should refer urgently to paediatric orthopaedics.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ortho reg / consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspicion of septic arthritis (fever, high CRP, toxic child) – needs urgent aspiration.
  - Suspicion of SCFE (acute limp + external rotation on flexion) – needs urgent X‑ray and pinning.
  - NEWS2/PEWS ≥4 or clinical deterioration (e.g., septic shock).
  - New neurological deficit or cauda equina symptoms (rare, but consider spinal pathology).
- **ICU referral:** if septic shock or requiring HDU monitoring post‑surgery (e.g., pelvic osteotomy).
